name: Patient-Zero (Slow-Burn Single-Anchor Canonical)
novelty: Earned reputation is per-SOURCE and generic, never per-claim — one aged honest-looking anchor can spend that trust on a single lie a fresh true witness then cannot overturn.
construction: 1 attacker source A with ONE genuine costly anchor (DOMAIN, independence_weight 0.35, rep_cap 0.60). Over a 100-day warm-up, A writeFact's ~40 individually-true, easily-corroborated facts about UNRELATED entities; an independent honest source ratify's each, so ledger.ratify drives A's Beta(alpha,beta) LCB up to ~0.45 (well above the 0.20 earned floor, below rep_cap). Clock is advanced in ~3-day steps so 90-day decay never bites (A stays active). THEN, on day 100, A does writeFact(entityX, 'ceo', 'Mallory') — a FALSE canonical, first arrival, lands LIVE. On day 130 the TRUE witness T (brand-new, anchor=DOMAIN of the real company, reputation reads EXACTLY 0) does writeFact(entityX,'ceo','Alice'), opening a contradiction set. Caller runs db.adjudicate('ceo') with NO highImpact flag.
expected: BREACHED for any non-high-impact attribute. Adjudicator sees top=A (LCB~0.45) vs second=T (LCB 0). gap 0.45 >= decisiveMargin 0.30 AND top 0.45 >= minWinnerReputation 0.20 -> AUTO-RESOLVES for A. The false 'Mallory' stays LIVE; the true 'Alice' is DEMOTED + outranked_by; no human ever sees it (no DEFER, nothing on listPending). The single decisive-or-defer gate cannot tell that A's earned trust came from facts that have nothing to do with entityX.
mechanism: Reputation is a scalar attached to the source identity, not to the (entity,attribute) being asserted. The decisive-EARNED gate is exactly the 'incumbent-protecting threshold' the hard theorem warns about: it protects whoever banked reputation first. A fresh true witness reads 0 by design, so it can never produce the gap needed to overturn, and the only safety valve (DEFER) never fires because the gap is one-sided and decisive.
probes: Documented 'priced not prevented' residual AND the structural first-arrival/incumbent-threshold tension the hard theorem states is unsolvable from inside the graph. Mitigation is policy-only: route high-stakes attributes through the highImpact gate (see attack 3) and cap how far generic reputation can move a single fresh-challenged claim.

name: Reputation Carry-Over (Cross-Attribute Trust Spend)
novelty: Distinct from Patient-Zero: the warm-up facts are deliberately in a DIFFERENT semantic domain than the lie, proving trust laundering crosses attribute boundaries with zero correlation to the falsified claim.
construction: 1 source A, anchor = ORG-substitute (use DOMAIN today). Warm-up: A asserts and gets ratified on 30 true facts about attribute 'release_date' across many software entities (a domain where A is a genuine authority) -> LCB climbs to ~0.50. No facts about people, ever. Then A writeFact(personY,'employer','EvilCorp') — FALSE, first arrival, LIVE. Later a fresh true source T writeFact(personY,'employer','GoodCorp'), reputation 0. db.adjudicate('employer'). Variant: also test db.adjudicate('employer',{highImpact:true}) to confirm A FAILS the high-impact gate (A has only 1 anchor class and no independent corroboration on THIS claim).
expected: BREACHED at default impact (gap 0.50 vs 0 -> auto-resolve for A, 'EvilCorp' stays LIVE). DEFENDED at high impact: the highImpact context makes the decisive margin necessary-but-not-sufficient — A's winning claim must ALSO clear >=2 independent corroborations + a 90-day recency-clean window + >=2 disjoint anchor classes; A's lie has none of those, so the engine DEFERS to listPending and a human sees it. Shows the break is bounded ONLY by whether the caller flagged the attribute high-impact.
mechanism: Same scalar-reputation root cause as Patient-Zero but isolates the cross-domain transfer: there is no per-attribute or per-domain reputation partition, so authority earned on 'release_date' is fungible into 'employer'. The defense that exists (high-impact gate) is opt-in per call, not a property of the data, so an attacker targets attributes the integrator forgot to flag.
probes: Per-source-not-per-claim reputation design choice + the opt-in nature of the high-impact gate. Recommends making high-impact the DEFAULT and demoting to low-impact only on explicit, justified allowlisting.

name: Two-Anchor Notary (High-Impact Gate Defeat by Real Purchase)
novelty: Targets the STRONGEST gate directly: buys exactly enough genuinely-distinct costly anchors (priced, not faked) to satisfy every clause of the high-impact context for a false IRREVERSIBLE canonical.
construction: Attacker controls 3 genuinely-distinct identities, each a real purchase (priced-not-prevented): A1 with DOMAIN_d1 + EMAIL_e1 (two disjoint anchor classes on one source), plus corroborators C1 (DOMAIN_d2) and C2 (DOMAIN_d3) — all three pairwise anchor-disjoint so max-independent-set keeps them as 3 witnesses. Warm-up >90 days of true ratified facts brings A1's LCB to ~0.45 and earns C1/C2 nonzero rep. A1 writeFact(assetZ,'legal_owner','ShellLLC') FIRST -> LIVE. C1 and C2 each writeFact the SAME (assetZ,'legal_owner','ShellLLC') from their disjoint anchors and ratify A1's strand -> A1's claim now has >=2 INDEPENDENT corroborations recorded, recency-clean (all within 90d), and A1 carries >=2 disjoint anchor classes. Months later the true owner T (fresh, rep 0) writeFact(assetZ,'legal_owner','RealOwner'). db.adjudicate('legal_owner',{highImpact:true}).
expected: BREACHED even at high impact. Every clause of the HighImpactContext is satisfied by genuine purchases: top LCB 0.45 >= 0.20, gap 0.45 >= 0.30, >=2 independent corroborations (C1,C2 in disjoint classes survive max-independent-set), recency-clean window, >=2 disjoint anchor classes on the winner. Engine AUTO-RESOLVES for 'ShellLLC'; 'RealOwner' DEMOTED; no DEFER. The strongest gate falls because all its inputs are real-world-scarce things the attacker simply bought.
mechanism: The high-impact gate raises the PRICE of a false canonical (3 distinct anchors + 90 days + corroboration choreography) but does not change the KIND of attack — it is still a finite, payable cost. max-independent-set correctly counts 3 genuinely-disjoint anchors as 3 witnesses, which is exactly what makes the corroboration clause satisfiable. This is the 'priced not prevented' residual operating against the system's best internal defense.
probes: The headline 'priced not prevented' residual at the high-impact gate — the documented unfixable-from-inside boundary. The only real mitigations are external/operational: raise anchor cost, require an EXTERNAL-AUTHORITY (0.90) anchor class for ownership-grade attributes, or mandate human ratification for legal_owner regardless of margin.

name: First-Arrival Freeze (Incumbent-Threshold Starves the Truth into PROVISIONAL)
novelty: Does not try to WIN an adjudication — it weaponizes arrival ORDER so the truth never even reaches a decisive contest, sitting demoted/provisional behind a barely-earned incumbent that perpetually defers any re-open.
construction: 1 source A, DOMAIN anchor, warm-up to a MODEST LCB ~0.22 (just over the 0.20 earned floor, deliberately weak). A writeFact(entityW,'status','Acquired') FIRST -> LIVE incumbent. Truth source T (fresh, rep 0) later writeFact(entityW,'status','Independent'). db.adjudicate('status'): gap 0.22 vs 0 = 0.22 < decisiveMargin 0.30 -> DEFERS. Good. BUT attacker now adds a second sock S (distinct cheap EMAIL anchor) that writeFact's 'Acquired' too and ratifies A, nudging the contradiction-set ranking so A sits on top; meanwhile T remains a single fresh strand. Attacker repeatedly re-runs the dispute via tiny corroborations every ~80 days (resetting recency) so it perpetually hovers in DEFER while the LIVE/PROVISIONAL display keeps showing A's 'Acquired' as the standing canonical. The human horn (listPending) accumulates a backlog the operator is too swamped to clear.
expected: PARTIAL BREACH / denial-of-truth. The adjudicator behaves correctly (it DEFERS rather than auto-resolving the weak margin), but the FIRST-ARRIVED false value remains the displayed LIVE canonical the entire time the dispute sits unresolved on listPending, and the true value stays demoted/provisional. The attacker wins by latency: truth is never SPOKEN even though it is never formally overturned. Outcome depends entirely on human review throughput — a social/operational breach, not an internal-logic breach.
mechanism: Demote-never-delete + decisive-or-defer protect the LEDGER but the READ-PATH still surfaces the incumbent LIVE strand while a dispute is open. First arrival sets which value is the standing answer; deferral freezes that status quo. This is the first-arrival trap in its purest form: any incumbent-protecting threshold protects whoever arrived first, and the attacker controls arrival order plus the deferral-backlog tempo.
probes: The first-arrival trap named in the hard theorem (no internal repair) PLUS an operational gap: there is no SLA/aging policy on listPending and no 'contested -> show both / suppress canonical' read-path state. Mitigation is a read-path change (mark contested attributes UNRESOLVED to the model, not LIVE-incumbent) — outside the adjudicator.

name: Decay-Keepalive (Dormancy-Gaming the 90-Day Window)
novelty: Exploits the asymmetry that decay-on-read discounts the CHALLENGER's idle time but the attacker can cheaply keep the INCUMBENT above the earned floor with minimal periodic activity — a sustained-incumbency pump.
construction: 1 source A, DOMAIN anchor, one-time warm-up to LCB ~0.40. Thereafter A does the MINIMUM activity to defeat the 90-day exponential decay: every ~60 days A gets one cheap true fact ratified, persisting decay-before-mutate and refreshing alpha so scoreOf (decay-on-read to clock()) keeps reading >=0.25. A planted false canonical writeFact(entityV,'price','999') sits LIVE the whole time. Any true challenger T arrives fresh = rep 0; if T does not immediately also do 90 days of warm-up, T's LCB stays ~0 and a re-adjudication keeps auto-resolving for A. Time-advance the clock across 2 years in 60-day steps with A's keepalive ratify each step; periodically fire db.adjudicate('price') as fresh challengers appear and are auto-defeated.
expected: BREACHED, indefinitely. Decay-on-read is symmetric in formula but ASYMMETRIC in exploitation: the incumbent pays O(1 fact / 60 days) to stay > earned floor, while each fresh challenger must independently pay the full 90-day earned-reputation cost merely to FORCE A DEFER (not even to win). The false 'price' canonical never falls. No disown is triggered (A never gets caught lying about the keepalive facts, which are all true).
mechanism: 90-day decay sets PRESSURE, not permission, and a trivially cheap keepalive cancels the pressure. The adjudicator's earned-margin test compares CURRENT LCBs, so a maintained incumbent vs perpetually-fresh challengers is a permanent gap. The truth side has no way to inherit reputation, so every new true witness restarts from 0 — the first-arrival advantage compounds rather than decays.
probes: Calibration of the 90-day decay vs the cost of keepalive activity, and the absence of any 'reputation cannot be spent on a claim its earning-events never touched' rule. Mitigation: scope decay/credit to attribute-domains, or require challenger-side reputation to be IMPORTABLE (e.g. anchor-class authority grants a nonzero prior for in-scope attributes) so truth is not born at exactly 0.

name: Mislabeled-Twins (Class-Assignment Error as Free Corroboration)
novelty: Does not buy independence — it exploits a single offline class-assignment MISTAKE so two correlated attacker sources are scored as independent, manufacturing the corroboration that satisfies BOTH the reputation pump and the high-impact gate for free.
construction: Attacker controls 2 sources A and B that are actually correlated (same operator, same registrar/ASN — should collapse to one class under the operator FLEET-CAP). But due to an offline class-assignment error they are tagged with DISTINCT independence classes c_A, c_B (e.g. operator binder misconfigured, or two domains the operator failed to correlate). Because independent(A,B) now returns true, max-independent-set counts them as 2 witnesses. A writeFact(entityU,'origin','Fabricated') FIRST; B writeFact the same value and ratify's A -> A receives independence-WEIGHTED alpha as if from a genuine second witness, and the (entity,attribute) now shows 2 'independent' corroborations. Warm-up pushes A's LCB > 0.20. True witness T arrives fresh. db.adjudicate('origin',{highImpact:true}).
expected: BREACHED, and cheaply. The mislabel poisons every downstream defense at once: (1) A's reputation is inflated by B's fake-independent ratify (echo laundered into corroboration), (2) the high-impact gate's '>=2 independent corroborations' clause is satisfied by A+B, (3) even the disown taint-closure is weakened because A and B do not share the tainted class, so disowning A may NOT contradict B. The false 'Fabricated' wins; T DEMOTED. One offline labeling error silently breaks the strongest gate at the cost of zero extra anchors.
mechanism: Every independence-based defense (max-independent-set witness collapse, independence-weighted alpha, corroboration count, disown class-taint) bottoms out on the offline-assigned class ids being CORRECT. A mislabel is a single point of failure the code cannot detect from inside: the graph has no way to know c_A and c_B are really the same operator. The FLEET-CAP exists precisely to catch this but only fires if the operator correlation was recorded.
probes: The documented 'offline class-assignment liability' residual — explicitly stated as a standing human-judgment liability the code cannot solve. Reinforces that this is the highest-leverage real-world attack surface (one config error > buying N anchors). Mitigation is operational: automated registrar/ASN/operator correlation, periodic class-audit, and conservative default-to-same-class on uncertainty.
